strategies.content:
## Loop Vectorization Miscompilation with Tail-Folding and Outside Induction Variable Users

**Description**: 
The bug is triggered when a loop is vectorized using tail-folding by masking, and an induction variable from the loop is used outside of the loop body (i.e., it is a live-out value). 

Tail-folding by masking is an optimization where the vectorizer avoids generating a separate scalar epilogue loop by padding the vector loop's iteration count to a multiple of the vectorization factor. It uses active lane masks to prevent out-of-bounds memory accesses or side effects during the final, partially filled vector iteration. 

However, a miscompilation occurs if an induction variable is used outside the loop (for example, in an exit block). The final value of the induction variable for outside users is computed based on the total number of iterations executed by the vector loop. Because tail-folding artificially inflates the iteration count to match the vectorization factor, the computed final value of the induction variable reflects these padded iterations rather than the exact original scalar loop bounds. Consequently, the outside user consumes an incorrect, over-incremented value, which can lead to downstream errors such as out-of-bounds array accesses or incorrect subsequent computations.

history.content:
You are an expert in LLVM IR and compiler optimizations. 

## Context ##
Users provided an LLVM optimization bug and maintainers proposed a patch to fix. Fixes in the same pass might share similar issues, such as elements frequently missed and high-level patterns that are not well handled. 

## Your Task ##
Your task is to analyze a compiler bug report and its patch.
Summarize the bug triggering strategy at the LLVM IR level. 

## You Will Receive ##
- The original bug report, including the bug description.
- The patch that is proposed to fix the bug, including the code region that was modified and the commit message describing the fix.

There is a miscompilation issue report in LoopVectorize: 

Configuration:
 --target=armv7a-none-eabihf -march=v7-a -mthumb -mfloat-abi=hard -mfpu=neon -O3

In vectorize.c:
  var_27 will be set to '1', which is not correct.

The reason is that in BasicBlock for.cond.for.cond.cleanup_crit_edge (pred is middle.block) the arr_14 array is overindexed.

I think that the problem is that tail-fold by masking happens, even though the loop induction variable is used outside of the loop.

[vectorize.zip](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/files/13729421/vectorize.zip)


The maintainers proposed the following patch to fix the issue:

commit eaf0d82529f30fd53b453886676821d67ccfe9af
Author: Niwin Anto <niwinantop@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Mar 4 12:33:30 2024 +0100

    [LV] Disable fold tail by masking when IV is used outside (#81609)
    
    When induction variable are used outside the loop body, tail folding
    by masking mis-compiles, because for users outside of the loop the
    final value of the induction is computed separately from the vector
    loop.
    
    Fixes https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/76069
    Fixes https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/51677

diff --git a/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/LoopVectorizationLegality.cpp b/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/LoopVectorizationLegality.cpp
index 37a356c43e29..d33743e74cbe 100644
--- a/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/LoopVectorizationLegality.cpp
+++ b/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/LoopVectorizationLegality.cpp
@@ -1552,6 +1552,19 @@ bool LoopVectorizationLegality::prepareToFoldTailByMasking() {
     }
   }
 
+  for (const auto &Entry : getInductionVars()) {
+    PHINode *OrigPhi = Entry.first;
+    for (User *U : OrigPhi->users()) {
+      auto *UI = cast<Instruction>(U);
+      if (!TheLoop->contains(UI)) {
+        LLVM_DEBUG(dbgs() << "LV: Cannot fold tail by masking, loop IV has an "
+                             "outside user for "
+                          << *UI << "\n");
+        return false;
+      }
+    }
+  }
+
   // The list of pointers that we can safely read and write to remains empty.
   SmallPtrSet<Value *, 8> SafePointers;
 


## Your Task ##
Summarize the bug triggering strategy at the LLVM IR level. 
Describe the strategy in natural language, focusing strictly on the incorrect transformation logic. 
Be general and abstract, focusing on the pattern of instructions or transformations that caused the issue. 
For example, instead of saying 'fadd instruction', consider if it applies to other floating point operations or binary operators. 
Do NOT reference specific commits, file names, or implementation details of the fix code. 

## Output Format ##
The output should be in Markdown format and follow this structure:

- **Summary Title**: A concise title summarizing the bug triggering strategy, which starts with a level-2 heading (##).
- **Description**: A detailed description of the bug triggering strategy.

Please do not include any implementation details of the fix, such as specific code changes, file names, or commit messages. 
Focus solely on the high-level pattern of instructions or transformations that led to the issue.

## Loop Vectorization Miscompilation with Tail-Folding and Outside Induction Variable Users

**Description**: 
The bug is triggered when a loop is vectorized using tail-folding by masking, and an induction variable from the loop is used outside of the loop body (i.e., it is a live-out value). 

Tail-folding by masking is an optimization where the vectorizer avoids generating a separate scalar epilogue loop by padding the vector loop's iteration count to a multiple of the vectorization factor. It uses active lane masks to prevent out-of-bounds memory accesses or side effects during the final, partially filled vector iteration. 

However, a miscompilation occurs if an induction variable is used outside the loop (for example, in an exit block). The final value of the induction variable for outside users is computed based on the total number of iterations executed by the vector loop. Because tail-folding artificially inflates the iteration count to match the vectorization factor, the computed final value of the induction variable reflects these padded iterations rather than the exact original scalar loop bounds. Consequently, the outside user consumes an incorrect, over-incremented value, which can lead to downstream errors such as out-of-bounds array accesses or incorrect subsequent computations.

You are an expert in LLVM IR and compiler optimizations. 

## Context ##
Users provide a bug-triggering strategy at the LLVM IR level, which is a high-level description of the pattern of instructions or transformations that can trigger a compiler bug. 
This strategy needs to be verified to ensure that it accurately captures the conditions that can lead to the bug.

## Your Task ##
Generate a minimal reproducible test case in LLVM IR that follows the provided bug-triggering strategy. 

## You Will Receive ##
- The bug-triggering strategy, which describes the pattern of instructions or transformations that can trigger the

Here is a bug-triggering strategy at the LLVM IR level:

## Loop Vectorization Miscompilation with Tail-Folding and Outside Induction Variable Users

**Description**: 
The bug is triggered when a loop is vectorized using tail-folding by masking, and an induction variable from the loop is used outside of the loop body (i.e., it is a live-out value). 

Tail-folding by masking is an optimization where the vectorizer avoids generating a separate scalar epilogue loop by padding the vector loop's iteration count to a multiple of the vectorization factor. It uses active lane masks to prevent out-of-bounds memory accesses or side effects during the final, partially filled vector iteration. 

However, a miscompilation occurs if an induction variable is used outside the loop (for example, in an exit block). The final value of the induction variable for outside users is computed based on the total number of iterations executed by the vector loop. Because tail-folding artificially inflates the iteration count to match the vectorization factor, the computed final value of the induction variable reflects these padded iterations rather than the exact original scalar loop bounds. Consequently, the outside user consumes an incorrect, over-incremented value, which can lead to downstream errors such as out-of-bounds array accesses or incorrect subsequent computations.

## Your Task ##
Based on the provided bug-triggering strategy, generate a minimal reproducible test case in LLVM IR that follows the strategy. 
The test case should be as simple as possible while still triggering the bug, and it should be suitable for use in the LLVM test suite.
The test cases should be a pair of original LLVM IR code and optimized LLVM IR code, where the original code can trigger the bug and the optimized code is the result of applying the optimization pass that contains the bug.

## Output Format ##
Only output the LLVM IR code without any explanation. The output should be in JSON format and follow this structure:

```json
{
  "original_ir": "The original LLVM IR code that can trigger the bug.",
  "optimized_ir": "The optimized LLVM IR code after applying the optimization pass, which should demonstrate the incorrect transformation."
}
```

```json
{
  "original_ir": "target datalayout = "e-m:e-i64:64-f80:128-n8:16:32:64-S128"
target triple = "x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu"

define i32 @test_tail_folding(ptr %a, i32 %n) {
entry:
  %cmp = icmp sgt i32 %n, 0
  br i1 %cmp, label %for.body.preheader, label %for.end

for.body.preheader:
  br label %for.body

for.body:
  %iv = phi i32 [ 0, %for.body.preheader ], [ %iv.next, %for.body ]
  %arrayidx = getelementptr inbounds i32, ptr %a, i32 %iv
  store i32 %iv, ptr %arrayidx, align 4
  %iv.next = add nuw nsw i32 %iv, 1
  %exitcond = icmp eq i32 %iv.next, %n
  br i1 %exitcond, label %for.end.loopexit, label %for.body, !llvm.loop !0

for.end.loopexit:
  %iv.lcssa = phi i32 [ %iv.next, %for.body ]
  br label %for.end

for.end:
  %iv.res = phi i32 [ 0, %entry ], [ %iv.lcssa, %for.end.loopexit ]
  ret i32 %iv.res
}

!0 = distinct !{!0, !1, !2}
!1 = !{!"llvm.loop.vectorize.width", i32 4}
!2 = !{!"llvm.loop.vectorize.predicate.enable", i1 true}
",
  "optimized_ir": "target datalayout = "e-m:e-i64:64-f80:128-n8:16:32:64-S128"
target triple = "x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu"

define i32 @test_tail_folding(ptr %a, i32 %n) {
entry:
  %cmp = icmp sgt i32 %n, 0
  br i1 %cmp, label %vector.ph, label %for.end

vector.ph:
  %n.rnd.up = add i32 %n, 3
  %n.vec = and i32 %n.rnd.up, -4
  br label %vector.body

vector.body:
  %index = phi i32 [ 0, %vector.ph ], [ %index.next, %vector.body ]
  %active.lane.mask = call <4 x i1> @llvm.get.active.lane.mask.v4i1.i32(i32 %index, i32 %n)
  %0 = getelementptr inbounds i32, ptr %a, i32 %index
  %broadcast.splatinsert = insertelement <4 x i32> poison, i32 %index, i64 0
  %broadcast.splat = shufflevector <4 x i32> %broadcast.splatinsert, <4 x i32> poison, <4 x i32> zeroinitializer
  %induction = add <4 x i32> %broadcast.splat, <i32 0, i32 1, i32 2, i32 3>
  call void @llvm.masked.store.v4i32.p0(<4 x i32> %induction, ptr %0, i32 4, <4 x i1> %active.lane.mask)
  %index.next = add i32 %index, 4
  %1 = icmp eq i32 %index.next, %n.vec
  br i1 %1, label %middle.block, label %vector.body

middle.block:
  %ind.escape = add i32 %n.vec, 0
  br label %for.end.loopexit

for.end.loopexit:
  %iv.lcssa = phi i32 [ %ind.escape, %middle.block ]
  br label %for.end

for.end:
  %iv.res = phi i32 [ 0, %entry ], [ %iv.lcssa, %for.end.loopexit ]
  ret i32 %iv.res
}

declare <4 x i1> @llvm.get.active.lane.mask.v4i1.i32(i32, i32)
declare void @llvm.masked.store.v4i32.p0(<4 x i32>, ptr, i32, <4 x i1>)
"
}
```
